content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Audience
Nội dung: [Audience]
This document should be available to all users of information systems provided by the Jardine Restaurant Group, regardless of their employment status within the company.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Audience

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Definition of Data Privacy
Nội dung: [Definition of Data Privacy]
Data privacy is the relationship between collection and dissemination of data, technology, the public expectation of privacy, and the legal and political issues surrounding them.
Privacy concerns exist wherever personally identifiable information is collected and stored – in digital form or otherwise. Improper or non-existent disclosure control can be the root cause for privacy issues.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Definition of Data Privacy

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Objective
Nội dung: [Objective]
The objective of this policy is to ensure that the Jardine Restaurant Group protects itself against losses arising from poor management or controls, inappropriate systems, unavailability or exposure of data, and breaches in regulation or legislation.
To achieve this, it is essential to protect the company's information assets from all threats, whether internal or external, deliberate or accidental. It is also necessary to assure business continuity and control business damage by preventing and minimising the impact of security incidents.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Objective

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Scope
Nội dung: [Scope]
Data Privacy in the company is trying to protect the Customer Personal Data Privacy Policy
Data Privacy Policy – this policy of customer personal data privacy and Employee information which is documented, approved and reviewed annually, which includes the following items
Obtaining of consent and notification of purposes with respect to the collection, use and disclosure of personal data
Use, retention and disposal of personal data
Provision of access to individuals for review and update of their personal data
Notification for disclosure of personal data to third parties
Physical and logical security protection of personal data from unauthorised access
Monitoring of compliance with defined privacy practices
Personal data incident handling
Data privacy risk assessment

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Scope

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Management Intent
Nội dung: [Management Intent]
The strength of a business is measured by the loyalty and size of its customer base combined with a track record and future roadmap for providing quality products and services. It is no surprise then that enterprises are so concerned with protecting their customer information and intellectual property. The repercussions of data leaks in any of these areas could result in competitors poaching customers from the data leak victim and going to market with a similar solution at lower cost. Even worse, publicity around a known data leak in these areas tarnishes the company brand and reputation and will almost certainly result in attrition of their customer base.
The results of such breaches can be far-reaching. Organizations must not only bear the expense of fines, remediation (e.g. credit monitoring, user notifications), but also any resulting loss of shareholder and customer confidence, impact on reputation, brand, stock value, and even potential criminal penalties. Dealing with a data security breach is disruptive to business and can give competitors an advantage. It does not matter whether breaches are accidental or deliberate; what matters is that the organization is perceived to have failed in its duty to care for personal and confidential information.
The company’s management is committed to the goals and principles of data privacy as set out in this policy and its supporting documents.
The IT Manager has approved the Data Privacy Policy and it has the support of the Jardine Restaurant Group’s Executive Committee. The IT Manager will maintain functional ownership of the policy document and all management-level staff will be responsible for ensuring that the requirements of the policy are brought to the attention of their staff (permanent or temporary) and any contractors or agency staff, and for monitoring its effective application. However individuals remain responsible for their own actions and those who are in any doubt should consult their manager. Failure to comply with requirements may lead to disciplinary action in certain instances and/or render the individual and the Jardine Restaurant Group’s liable to prosecution.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Management Intent

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Policies and Supporting Documents
Nội dung: [Policies and Supporting Documents]
The complete Jardine Restaurant Group Data Privacy Policy can be divided into three layers of documentation:
Policies: High-level statements of objectives and intent, indicating management direction and support.
Standards: Statements of security control, intent and direction which enable security to be controlled.
Procedures: Collections of guidelines, checklists and tools that enable the specified security controls to be implemented.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Policies and Supporting Documents

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Security Policy
Nội dung: [Security Policy]
It is the policy of the Jardine Restaurant Group to ensure that confidentiality of information will be assured, integrity of information will be maintained, and availability of information to authorised personnel will be preserved, through a systematic and measured application of control measures. Each part of the organisation will accordingly implement strategies to deliver effective controls in line with the guidance available in the supporting documents. Application of control measures will be subject to both internal and external audit for reasons of quality control and legal compliance.
The various sections of this document will satisfy the following goals:
Define the acceptable and unacceptable uses of the Jardine Restaurant Group’s information systems. Security breaches often occur when systems are used for inappropriate purposes.
Prevent unauthorised access to any of the Jardine Restaurant Group’s systems, whether from inside or outside the trusted networks; and hence protect the Jardine Restaurant Group’s information from being released to those not authorised to possess it, and maintain trust that the information available is complete and accurate.
Ensure that the Jardine Restaurant Group’s systems - in particular, those critical to all aspects of running the business - are available at all times, and are protected from service interruptions, whether through malicious attacks or through accidents or disasters.
Ensure compliance with legislative and contractual obligations, and internal quality requirements.
Set down the actions to be followed in the event of a suspected or actual breach of security.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023

Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities
Nội dung: [Roles and Responsibilities]

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > IT Manager and Company Board
Nội dung: [IT Manager and Company Board]
The Board, along with the IT Manager, are responsible for setting clear company policy. This involves:
confirming the need for data privacy policy and the necessary resources required
defining clear responsibilities and accountabilities for data privacy security
stressing that measures should always be proportionate to the assessed risks
ensuring regular monitoring and reporting of data privacy incidents
ensuring clear and simple security standards are developed, and followed by employees

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > IT Manager and Company Board

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > Data Privacy Committee
Nội dung: [Data Privacy Committee]
Data Privacy Committee, consists of Managers & Managers of Operations, Marketing, Finance and IT Departments, are responsible for monitoring & execution of data privacy policy. This involves:
defining the procedure to execute the data privacy policy
monitoring the execution of data privacy policy
performing regular review on the execution of data privacy policy
handling of daily process related to data privacy issue
identifying the reason of data privacy incident and reporting for data privacy incident to Company Board
executing of actions from Company Broad in case of data privacy incident

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > Data Privacy Committee

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > IT Team
Nội dung: [IT Team]
The IT Team is responsible for ensuring overall compliance through advice, measurement and incident investigation. This involves:
co-ordinating data privacy security awareness campaigns to educate all staff
offering guidance to managers on carrying out data privacy security risk assessments, security reviews, and self-assessments
offering guidance to ensure that appropriate physical security measures are taken for the protection of offices, data centres, server and communication rooms
supporting and approving reviews of the security requirements of major new facilities
co-ordinating and facilitating self-assessment programmes and gathering data
working with managers to investigate reported potential weaknesses in data privacy security issue
constantly reviewing and revising standards and procedures documents

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > IT Team

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > Departmental Representatives
Nội dung: [Departmental Representatives]
Departmental Representatives (Departmental Managers, IT Co-ordinators, Data Protection Administrators) are responsible for helping colleagues comply with requirements. This involves:
undertaking, with colleagues, a risk assessment to ascertain the sensitivity of customer data information handled
ensuring that processes are in place to safeguard personal data in accordance with the relevant Privacy Ordinance
ensuring that appropriate physical security measures are taken for the protection of IT equipment
arranging training in line with the level of skill and knowledge required by users

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > Departmental Representatives

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Data Privacy Policy > Roles and Responsibilities > Individual Staff Members
Nội dung: [Individual Staff Members]
Individual Staff Members (permanent and temporary) are responsible for complying with the requirements of the Data Privacy Security Policy. This involves:
reading Data Privacy Security Notices and taking action to comply with requirements
being security-aware at all times
adopting a clear desk policy and following good housekeeping practices
protecting information in accordance with its sensitivity
understanding incident reporting procedures
participating in risk assessments and security reviews
only using authorised licensed computer software and hardware

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023

Ngữ cảnh: Detail for Data Privacy Policy > Data Category > JARDINE RESTAURANT GROUP
Nội dung: [JARDINE RESTAURANT GROUP]
PIZZA HUT VIET NAM PRIVACY POLICY
Pizza Hut Viet Nam (“Pizza Hut”, “we”, “our” or “us”) takes your privacy very seriously and wishes to assure you that your personal information will be treated as confidential. This is how we will manage information we collect from you through our websites and online services, including pizzahut.vn, Pizza Hut mobile application and any other owned or operated websites and online services that link to this Privacy Policy (collectively the “Sites”) and through our restaurants/outlets (“Services”). We will follow the standards of personal data privacy protection in complying with the requirements of the Personal Data protection at Decree 13 (VN regulation) as amended from time to time.

Nội dung: [1. Types of Data we collect]
When you use our Sites and/or Services, we may collect from you the following information which may contain personal data as defined under the Decree 13 (VN regulation) (“Data”):
- your personal information such as your name, address, mobile phone number, birthday (date & month) and email address;
- your photos which are collected via our Facebook page; and
- optional information about your occupation, age, gender, number of family members, age group for family members, marital status and monthly household income.

Use of Personal Data
For Online Ordering, we will use your email address for security reasons to confirm that you are who you say you are when you log in to our Online Ordering service. We may use your email address, or your response to any online survey, to notify you of future Pizza Hut promotions or events. You can choose not to receive these notifications if you wish. We will use your personal information to fulfil purchases using the Online Ordering service and, if necessary, to process your credit facilities.
We may use other companies to perform services on our behalf, including fulfilling purchases using the Online Ordering service and sending emails. These other companies may be supplied with or have access to your personal information solely for the purpose of providing these services on our behalf.
The non-personally identifiable information can be used to evaluate how our customers use and navigate our website, including the number of visitors and the frequency and length of their visits, in order to improve the quality of our service.
For Call Centre and Store Level, only Customer Name with contact number, and delivery address related information had been stored, which is used for delivery service only. No personal data had been stored at Call Centre and Store Level.
For employees, only name, address, date of birth, Employment history, Contact information, Bank details for payroll.
Retention and Disposal of Personal Data
For Customer Data at Online Platform, we’ll retain Customer Data at the reasonable time at our Customer Database stored at Computer Server in Data Centre. Also, we’ll dispose the Customer Data for NOT purchased after a specific time interval.
For Customer Data at Call Centre, we’ll retain this kind of Customer Data at Call Centre Customer Database stored at Computer Server at Call Centre.
For Store Level, we’ll retain Customer Data at Store’s POS Server for daily operation used, his / her personal data will be removed at next day (for the customer with advance order, his / her personal data will be removed at next day after the delivery date of the advance order)
For employee data, Personal data will be retained only for as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes for which it was collected or to comply with legal requirements.

Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Objectives
Nội dung: [Objectives]
To define what is meant by PHV’s network perimeter and security
To specify the requirements that must be in place for the perimeter to be a strong and reliable boundary between various untrusted networks and the PHV trusted network.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Objectives

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Scope
Nội dung: [Scope]
This standard concerns the following areas of the PHV information system:
Network architecture - how networks with various degrees of trust are interconnected, and safe locations for servers and network devices
Device configuration - appropriate and inappropriate traffic
Access control lists - acceptable and unacceptable rules; how to create new rules, change existing rules, delete rules, and ensure that the live rule-base is consistent with approved requests
Use of application layer gateways to mediate traffic
Event logging and intruder detection systems

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Scope

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Roles and Responsibilities
Nội dung: [Roles and Responsibilities]
The IT Manager is responsible for studying network infrastructure requirements, proposing modifications and solutions, and evaluating those solutions and presenting them to IT management.
The IT Manager are responsible for reviewing the needs for modifications and solutions to the network infrastructure, and approving the implementation procedures for such modifications.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Roles and Responsibilities

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Definitions
Nội dung: [Definitions]
ACL (Access Control List): A list of rules on a network device, such as, a router or a firewall that is used for granting or denying permission for a host or network to have access to another host or network. The ACL can usually include protocol information (for example, IP, TCP, and ICMP) and service information (for example, world-wide web).
DMZ: A segment of network between two firewalls, or on the third interface of a single firewall. This can be considered a semi-trusted network, and is a staging area for inbound and outbound traffic that cannot be trusted to cross the perimeter directly.
Firewall: Network device with at least two interfaces, all of which are Ethernet. By using a user-defined table of rules, traffic between the connected networks is strictly controlled. A firewall may also include application-layer gateways (proxies) and an intruder detection system (IDS).
Perimeter: All devices that separate the PHV network from other networks. For example, routers and firewalls separate the PHV network from the Internet or other third-party networks.
Proxies: Servers that pass traffic across a network perimeter on behalf of other systems, at the application layer. Examples include e-mail proxies (which proxy in both directions) and web proxies (which generally only proxy outbound traffic). These can be standalone, or embedded in a firewall. Their purpose is to remove the need for direct connections across the network perimeter.
Router: Network device with at least two interfaces, at least one of which is Ethernet. Its purpose is to direct and control traffic between the connected networks.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Definitions

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Definitions > Semi-trusted network: A network (for example, a DMZ) that is accessible from an untrusted network, but such accesses are mediated and controlled.
Nội dung: [Semi-trusted network: A network (for example, a DMZ) that is accessible from an untrusted network, but such accesses are mediated and controlled.]
Trusted network: A network with a defined perimeter, which is controlled entirely by the network owners. Perimeter devices must be well configured, and access to the network from outside will be extremely limited. Access to the network is granted only to a known population of users.
Untrusted network: An unprotected network with an undefined population of users, for example, the Internet. Most third-party private networks are considered to be untrusted, because their perimeters are not under PHV control.
VPN (virtual private network): By using a combination of access control and encryption, it is possible to create a tunnel between a number of networks in such a way that they appear to be a single entity. The tunnelled traffic may safely cross an untrusted network (such as the Internet) providing that sufficiently strong encryption is in use.
Ports: The only inbound protocols (i.e. those that originate on the Internet) that are routinely permitted through the group’s firewalls are SMTP, POP3, IMAP, HTTP, HTTPS and SCP , and traffic is permitted only to appropriate servers. IT not allowed to enable insecure protocols(FTP, FTP-Data, telnet and TFTP) for each system and deny all to insecure protocol.
Store Password System: Cyber-Ark's is automated access control system and digial vaults are based on Patented Vault Technology™ Securing data from end-to-end using multiple security layers, including Firewall, Data Access Control and End-to-End Encryption. If remote access to system, need to IT Manager approval through Cyber – ’s system.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Definitions > Semi-trusted network: A network (for example, a DMZ) that is accessible from an untrusted network, but such accesses are mediated and controlled.

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Architecture
Nội dung: [Architecture]
All networks must be Internet Protocol (IP) version 4. This document applies only to such networks. Additional requirements will apply to any IPv6 environments that are installed.
The purpose of network security devices is to ensure that the PHV trusted network remains trusted at all times, and that access to the network is available only to those who are permitted to access it. For this reason, a strong security device must be present at every point of the network perimeter, and those devices must be wholly under the control of PHV.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Architecture

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Architecture > Location of Hosts
Nội dung: [Location of Hosts]
It is strongly recommended that no hosts are located on untrusted network segments outside the firewall. Hosts that are to accept connections from an untrusted network should be located on a DMZ connected to a third firewall port. It is a good idea (but not mandatory) to locate hosts mediating trusted-to-untrusted traffic on a DMZ – which, if possible, should be a different DMZ to those handling the inbound traffic. All other hosts should be on the trusted network.
Hosts facing semi-trusted or untrusted networks must be hardened to the greatest extent possible at both the operating system and application layers. This is detailed in the supporting procedures for operating system and application builds.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Architecture > Location of Hosts

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Architecture > Network Address Translation (NAT)
Nội dung: [Network Address Translation (NAT)]
NAT is the mapping of one or more IP addresses to a single IP address and is usually performed by a firewall or router. In static NAT, a single IP address is mapped to a single IP address; in dynamic (also known as “hide”) NAT, a range of IP addresses is mapped to a single address. NAT allows a trusted network to use an RFC1918 address range (such as .0/8) which cannot be routed across the Internet. The principal security advantage of this is that individual hosts on the trusted network do not have IP addresses that are meaningful in any way to an attacker on an untrusted network.
Dynamic NAT is a highly desirable feature for implementation on all external firewall interfaces connected to untrusted networks, and should be implemented. Inbound traffic directed to specific hosts (for example, on a DMZ) should be connected using static NAT. Infrequently, some application functionality may be incompatible with NAT, in which case, the application should be recoded or redesigned so that NAT functionality can be supported.

Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Access Control Lists (for Routers, Firewalls and Other Devices)
Nội dung: [Access Control Lists (for Routers, Firewalls and Other Devices)]

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Access Control Lists (for Routers, Firewalls and Other Devices)

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Access Control Lists (for Routers, Firewalls and Other Devices) > Everything that is not explicitly permitted is denied.
Nội dung: [Everything that is not explicitly permitted is denied.]
The default configuration on all security devices must be to deny all traffic. Where traffic is permitted, it must be because a rule has been added stating that it has been permitted. Denial of traffic is the default for most firewalls. On the other hand, most routers, especially Cisco routers, permit traffic unless it is explicitly denied in the device’s access control list (ACL).
Firewalls must have a safety net rule at the end of the rule table that denies all traffic from all sources, to all destinations, on all services and review firewall and Router sets at least every six months.
When creating and updating the ACLs in a firewall or router, the following standards must be taken into account:
It is mandatory that no rule should permit traffic to flow directly from an untrusted network to a trusted network. It is strongly recommended that no rule should permit traffic to flow from a trusted network directly to an untrusted network. Instead, traffic should be mediated either through a DMZ (semi-trusted network) or through a proxy. There are very few occasions where this might cause a business problem, and most of these can be corrected through the use of a plug-proxy.
Rules must be specific rather than general. Source addresses, destination addresses, and services must be specified as accurately as possible. Specifications using large subnets, and ‘any’ or ‘all’ statements must be avoided, unless they are actually required.
 
Example 1: The firewall is required to allow any host on the Internet to connect to the mail server. In this case, an ‘any’ statement is permitted. 

Example 2: A third-party network is permitted to access an extranet web server; the network addresses must be specified as accurately as possible; an ‘any’ statement is inappropriate in this case.
An IP address must be used in preference to a host name, where individual hosts are specified in rules. Firstly, this reduces the load on the device that must perform name lookups, if a fully-qualified domain name is specified. More significantly, using a host name shifts the security away from the security device itself and onto the DNS, which could be weaker, or even administered by a third-party outside PHV’s control. Changing the name-to-address mapping on the DNS effectively causes an update of the ACL, without any need to change information held on the security device.
Rules must be ordered from most specific to least specific. This ensures that no rules are over-ridden because of a more general preceding rule. Some firewalls have a facility for analysing the rule base for this kind of misconfiguration; if available, this facility must be used.
ACLs must also be stringently applied to outbound traffic. A common error is for a filtering router or firewall to be configured to carefully control and mediate traffic flowing into the trusted network, but to permit all traffic flowing outbound under the misapprehension that, because it originates internally, it cannot be harmful or malicious. In practice, a loose filtering policy of this type can be extremely useful for an attacker who has managed to gain tenuous control of a host on the trusted network. For this reason, it is mandatory that ACLs are applied as stringently to outbound traffic as they are to inbound traffic.
Special care must be taken when considering rules for UDP traffic. UDP is not ‘stateful’, and therefore is not a safe protocol like TCP. Most firewalls create an artificial state table for UDP, but in general filtering routers cannot do this. Care must be taken when implementing UDP protocols, because they cannot be safely plug-proxied in the same manner as TCP. For protocols such as SNMP, NTP and DNS, lookups must not be permitted to pass from an untrusted to a trusted network.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Activity Logging
Nội dung: [Activity Logging]
All devices with an interface on an untrusted or semi-trusted network must be capable of keeping logs of various types of activity. So that the logs have evidential value, they should be created using an industry-standard logging tool (for example, syslog or Windows Event Viewer). Logs must be copied to secure storage regularly - at least once a week - and at the same time, the clock on the device must be checked for accuracy, and the results recorded.
The problem with logging is that it often generates enormous amounts of data, and it becomes difficult for staff to search the logs for evidence of illicit activity. For this reason, it is rarely necessary to monitor all activity on devices such as routers and firewalls. Instead, the logging system should be configured to monitor exceptional activity only. A firewall, for example, does not need to keep track of all permitted connections through it. It should, however, log all denied connections, because these are indicative of either a misconfiguration or an attempted attack. All failed attempts at authentication must be logged, as must account lockouts caused by repeated failures.
Certain types of normal event must be logged. These include the addition and deletion of users, and any changes of password, successful or otherwise.
Logging attempted intrusions is highly recommended.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Activity Logging

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Logical Security Protection Policy (Network) > Intruder Detection Systems (IDS) and Intruder Prevention Systems (IPS)
Nội dung: [Intruder Detection Systems (IDS) and Intruder Prevention Systems (IPS)]
There are two types of IDS: network-based and host-based. A network-based IDS can detect potentially malicious traffic across a range of machines, by watching traffic on the wire. A host-based IDS can pick up attacks only against the host on which it is running, by monitoring traffic being received by the host’s network interfaces. These devices may be stand-alone, or may be integrated into a unified threat manager (UTM). The following requirements apply equally to either platform.
It is recommended that some form of IDS be in use to protect perimeter systems between untrusted and trusted networks. Most firewalls come with a simple integrated host-based IDS, and this can be used to detect certain types of attacks. Attacks on closed or filtered ports do not need to be logged. It is generally accepted that the outside of the network will come under almost constant scrutiny from automated attack tools, and hence most scans and sweeps can safely be ignored. Similarly, many worms probe certain ports on random IP addresses (such as TCP port 445, searching for exploitable Windows SMB vulnerabilities), and for this reason many denied connections can be ignored.
Incidents that should be logged are:
Attempted denial of service attacks, for example, SYN flood attacks.
Repeated attempts to guess a password, either manually or through a brute-force or dictionary attack.
Attempts to connect to the firewall itself, rather than to hosts protected by it - especially on known firewall management ports.
All these types of incidents are indicative of a focused attack rather than random probing. However, these types of incident are still relatively low priority, because they reflect attempted but unsuccessful attempts to compromise PHV’s security.
It is strongly recommended that network-based IDSs be deployed on any DMZs, and on the segment of network on the trusted side of any firewalls. These should be set to log all suspicious traffic. Any such events on these networks indicate that an attack has taken place and - to some extent - has been successful. Host-based IDSs should be deployed on critical servers - most likely to be on DMZs - that are accessed from untrusted networks. They too should be configured to log incidences of all appropriate attacks. Manual reviews of these logs should take place daily, with suspicious events being investigated immediately.
It is strongly recommended that the requirement for host- and network-based IDSs on any trusted networks used by PHV be evaluated. In particular, servers handling highly sensitive or confidential information may benefit from a host-based IDS.

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Personal Data Incident Handling > Objective
Nội dung: [Objective]
The purpose of this procedure is to ensure that PHV’s exposure to the impact of personal data security incidents is minimised. This will be achieved through setting standards for the recognition or detection and subsequent reporting of security incidents, and for the handling of such incidents. The procedure will specify standards for an incident response team. In case further investigation or escalation to law enforcement agencies is required, standards for evidence collection will be set.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Personal Data Incident Handling > Objective

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Personal Data Incident Handling > Definitions
Nội dung: [Definitions]
The key definition in this procedure concerns PHV’s corporate definition of an customer data security incident. The general definition shall be, “Any real or suspected adverse event affecting or likely to affect the confidentiality, integrity or availability of any of PHV’s information systems; or, any violation of PHV’s Information Security Policy”.
Examples of such incidents include:
Leakage of Customer Data at Store Level
Attempts (failed or successful) to gain unauthorised access to a system or its data.
Unauthorised use of a system for the processing or storage of data.

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Personal Data Incident Handling > Incident Handling
Nội dung: [Incident Handling]
From time to time even the most well protected organisation will be the victim of some kind of security incident. The key to minimising the impact of such incidents lies in:
The rapid detection of the fact that a breach has taken place.
The rapid mobilisation of an incident response team to analyse the problem.
A suitable response being formulated and implemented.
Incident handling falls into three functions:
Incident reporting – a central point of contact must be available at all times (24 hours a day, 7 days a week) so that reports can be collected and correlated.
Incident analysis – the central intelligence point will use reports to identify trends and patterns of intruder activity and recommend the corresponding preventative measures - the scope, priority, and threat of the incident driving the response.
Incident response – the central intelligence point may send out recommendations for recovery, containment and prevention, to tackle a minor threat; alternatively for a potentially major incident, it may be necessary for the experts to perform these tasks themselves.
The incident handling process must be reviewed after every incident, through self-criticism and detailed debriefing, to ensure that any perceived problems do not recur, and to identify areas where processes can be improved. This is essential because the speed with which an organisation can recognise, analyse, and respond to an incident will limit the damage and lower the cost of recovery.

metadata.source: JRG PHV Data Privacy Policy v2023
metadata.hierarchy: Personal Data Incident Handling > Incident Handling

content:
Ngữ cảnh: Personal Data Incident Handling > Procedure on Handling Incidents
Nội dung: [Procedure on Handling Incidents]
For Class 1 Incidents – Leakage of customer data at store level, the incident case reported by Store Manager, the reporting line will be described as follow.
Store Manager reported the incident case of customer data leakage to Area Coach / Operation Manager, AC /  will analysis the influent related to the case and reported to Customer Service Team. Customer Service Team will work with Data Privacy Committee - Marketing Public Relation Team to establish the contingency plan to facing public awareness, and also reported this case to Management Team. Management Team will make final decision of contingency plan to let frontline staff, customer service team and marketing team for execution.
For Class 2 Incidents – Leakage of customer data at computer system level, the incident case reported by IT or other parties, the reporting line will be described as follow.
IT or other parties reported the incident case of customer data leakage from computer system to IT CSIRT, CSIRT will check the validity of leakage from computer system. Once confirmed, CSIRT will report this case to IT Manager. IT Manager will instruct CSIRT to solve the incident case with immediate execution. On the other hand, IT Manager will report this incident to Data Privacy Committee - Customer Service Team and Marketing Public Relation Team. CS / MKT PR team will establish the contingency plan to facing public awareness, and also reported this case to Management Team with IT Manager. IT Manager will work with Management Team to make final decision of contingency plan to let frontline staff, customer service team and marketing team for execution.
For Detail, please refer to IT Information Security Policy.

Nội dung: [Incident Analysis and Review]

Within Risk Assessment section, we are basically working from different aspects to analyze of each Customer Data Storage Area.
